suggested_addition: Add to D7: 'Log training wall-clock time, peak memory (RSS), and generation/eval times per rung. Emit a `COST|` line in run_loop output with these metrics so experiments are self-documenting for cost analysis.'
source_excerpts: D7 — Capacity ladder + demand-driven scaling
Record signals + decision per rung.

title: OOM failures waste compute with no pre-flight memory estimation
description: The ladder relies on process SIGKILL (exit 137) to detect OOM, which means a training step may run for minutes before exceeding memory and being killed, wasting that compute. A lightweight memory pre-check could reject a rung before the expensive training attempt.
rationale: Wasted GPU/CPU cycles are direct costs. A rough memory model (e.g., num_params * bytes + batch * tile * hidden) could prevent futile launches, especially on resource-limited machines where OOM is likely.
suggested_addition: Before launching a training round for a new rung, run a quick memory estimation: compute expected memory from model parameter count, batch size, and tile count. If > available memory, reject with 'rung rejected (OOM risk)' and skip training, saving the compute.
source_excerpts: OOM/failure handling (FND-0010/0011): the train rung runs under a memory cap + wall-clock timeout; an abnormal exit → "rung rejected (train failed, likely OOM); micro-batching = future work"
micro-batching = future work (future work); edge-list emission; inference circuit-breaker/telemetry

title: No wall-time budget cap per experiment, risking runaway costs
target_section: TL;DR — how this solves the ask
description: The experiment budget is held constant only in 'rounds=8, gen-games/round=16, eval-games/round=80'. However, the wall time for a round can vary dramatically between small and large rungs (e.g., 2× larger model may run 4× slower per step). Without a time budget, an experiment could consume vastly more compute than the v3 baseline, undermining the 'budget held constant' claim in practical terms.
rationale: FinOps cares about actual compute cost, not just 'number of rounds'. If the experiment takes 10× the wall time of v3, that's effectively a 10× cost increase, even if the round count is identical. A time cap guardrails this.
suggested_addition: Add a non-negotiable constraint: 'Maximum experiment wall-clock time = 3× the v3 rich-critic Medium run's wall time (from prior logs). If any rung would exceed this under linear extrapolation of per-round time, scale-down or exit early.' Log total time at experiment end and report alongside the z-test.
source_excerpts: Budget held constant := identical (rounds=8, gen-games/round=16, eval-games/round=80, opponent=RandomPolicy, fixed seeds, + a 200-game final eval) as the v3 rich-critic Medium run (FND-0030/0017).
A null/negative result is an accepted, reported outcome (the user accepted the confound), not a ship-blocker

title: Throughput guard may be gamed by cheap baseline, masking expensive models
description: The guard rejects a rung if its throughput is <70% of the heuristic baseline turns/s. If the heuristic baseline is trivially fast (sub-millisecond per decision), a model that is 5× slower might still pass the guard, yet multiply the total experiment cost by 5×. The 70% threshold is arbitrary and decoupled from actual cost per decision.
rationale: A FinOps review asks: what is the cost per unit of work? Trading a tiny performance gain for a 3× slowdown would be flagged. The guard should enforce a maximum absolute cost per inference (e.g., ms/decision < X ms) rather than a relative one that can be satisfied by an extremely cheap baseline.
suggested_addition: Supplement the 70% relative gate with an absolute ms/decision ceiling derived from prior v3 rich-critic runs (e.g., max 5 ms/decision on target hardware). If any rung exceeds that absolute threshold, reject regardless of relative score. This couples the guard to real compute constraints, not an artificial baseline.
source_excerpts: D8 — Throughput guard (extends existing SimBenchmark): … REJECT a rung < 70% of the heuristic baseline turns/s.
SimBenchmark already exists (10.7 KB) … material change: the benchmark was added to measure the heuristic baseline turns/s

title: No cost-benefit analysis for added encoder capacity
target_section: TL;DR — how this solves the ask
description: The plan invests embeddings, GNN, self-attention, and cross-attention into the encoder but never specifies what cost increase is acceptable for what performance gain. The experiment only checks if it 'beats v3 on Medium,' but if it wins by a tiny margin while costing 10× more compute, that might not be a worthwhile investment from a FinOps perspective.
rationale: FinOps evaluates cost proportional to value. Without an upfront statement of tolerable cost increase, the team risks building an expensive solution that barely clears the statistical bar, while a simpler (and cheaper) encoder might achieve nearly the same result.
suggested_addition: Define a secondary cost-efficiency metric: 'Max acceptable cost multiplier = 2× the v3 rich-critic's total compute (wall-time×cores). If the winning rung exceeds that multiplier, the experiment is marked as 'cost-inefficient' and the recommendation is to investigate cheaper optimizations before adoption.'
source_excerpts: Pre-registered, falsifiable success metric (AC1): the structured encoder BEATS the v3 rich-pool on Medium at p<0.05…
Risk 4: v4 worse on Medium under from-scratch training … budget held constant

title: Hex clock-order convention asserts neighbor 0 = (+1,+1) without engine-tile-edge basis
description: The brief freezes a clock order `[(+1,+1),(0,+1),(-1,0),(-1,-1),(0,-1),(+1,0)]` and claims it mirrors `getIfTileExistsOrNull` branch A/B/C/D, but Unciv's true hex adjacency is defined by `HexMath.clockPositionToHexcoordMap` (12/0,2,4,6,8,10) and `tile.neighbors`/`getNeighborTiles`. The brief explicitly chooses NOT to use `tile.neighbors` and instead re-derives offsets — but `getIfTileExistsOrNull` is a generic coord lookup, NOT the hex adjacency definition. There is no stated proof that walking these 6 offsets reproduces the domain's actual notion of 'adjacent tile.'
rationale: If the 6 offsets don't match Unciv's real hex adjacency (which is the whole domain concept the GNN is supposed to encode), the model learns spatial relations that don't correspond to in-game adjacency — the encoder restores the wrong locality and AC1 tests a confounded hypothesis.
suggested_addition: Add a domain anchor: state that the 6 offsets are derived from `HexMath.clockPositionToHexcoordMap` (the engine's adjacency definition), and that the engine-fidelity test (D10 test 3) must compare against `tile.neighbors`/`getNeighborTiles` semantics — NOT merely `getIfTileExistsOrNull` over hand-picked offsets — to prove the gathered neighbors ARE the game's adjacent tiles.
source_excerpts:
for each
tile×direction (clock order `[(+1,+1),(0,+1),(-1,0),(-1,-1),(0,-1),(+1,0)]`) computes (nx,ny), direct lookup, then
world-wrap retry

via real `getIfTileExistsOrNull` over the 6 offsets in clock order (sentinel+mask0 for null) — **NOT `tile.neighbors`**
(null-filtered, loses degree-6 slots)

title: Entity↔node fusion uses zeroBasedIndex but visibility/fog rules differ between spatial and tokens
target_section: D4 — Self-attention over entity sets + entity↔node join
description: D4 fuses each entity (unit/city token) with its co-located GNN node embedding via tile-index. But the spatial channels are gated by visibility (`if(!visible) continue` skips owner/units/improvement), while opponent unit/city tokens may be emitted under different visibility/espionage rules. An opponent unit token may point to a tile node whose features were never populated (fog), so the fused node embedding is the sentinel/empty row — silently grounding the entity on a blank tile.
rationale: The domain has asymmetric information (fog of war, espionage); fusing an entity with a node that was zeroed by visibility rules misrepresents what the acting civ actually knows, corrupting the very 'grounding on the board' D4 claims to provide.
suggested_addition: Specify the visibility contract for entity↔node fusion: state whether opponent entity tokens are only emitted for visible/known tiles, and what the fused node embedding represents when the co-located tile is fogged (e.g., a fog node carries terrain/coords only). Add a test that an opponent unit on a fogged tile fuses with a node consistent with the acting civ's knowledge, not a contradiction.
source_excerpts:
gather the entity's co-located GNN node embedding
via its tile-index (D1.3) and concat/add into the entity token — grounding the entity on the board
out[base + 7] = (tile.getOwner()...  // after  if (!visible) continue

title: effWrapRadius for rectangular maps assumes width/2 == wrap distance — domain check missing
description: The brief pre-resolves `effWrapRadius = if(shape==rectangular) width/2 else radius`. But Unciv's world-wrap is a property of the map (`worldWrap` flag) and the wrap distance for rectangular maps depends on the actual grid layout. The brief asserts this equals `width/2` to mirror `getIfTileExistsOrNull`, but rectangular Unciv maps use offset/hex coordinates where the x-extent and the wrap modulus are not trivially `width/2`.
rationale: If the wrap radius is wrong for any rectangular Medium map, neighbor lookups across the wrap seam resolve to the wrong tile or to none, breaking adjacency exactly at the map edge — and the Medium experiment (the headline AC1) runs on Medium maps where this matters most.
suggested_addition: Confirm against the engine that `TileMap.getIfTileExistsOrNull` for rectangular world-wrap maps uses `radius = width/2` (cite the exact source line), and add to the adjacency-parity test (D10 test 2) a rectangular world-wrap map whose `width/2 != radius` with seam tiles, asserting the Python builder resolves the wrapped neighbor to the same tile as the live engine.
source_excerpts: **pre-resolved** `effWrapRadius = if(shape==rectangular) width/2 else radius`
radius=mapSize.radius (rectangular: width/2)

title: AC1 'beats v3 on Medium' is confounded by undertraining — success metric may be untestable
description: The brief candidly states the recorded v2 diagnosis attributes the Medium regression to undertraining under from-scratch-per-round training, and warns adding capacity could make Medium WORSE — yet AC1 pre-registers 'structured beats v3 on Medium' as the success metric while leaving the training regime untouched. The business question ('does restoring 2D locality help?') is structurally entangled with the training-regime confound the team chose not to fix.
rationale: From a domain standpoint, a pre-registered metric that the team's own root-cause analysis predicts will likely fail for reasons unrelated to the hypothesis under test is a weak experiment — a null result won't distinguish 'locality doesn't help' from 'undertraining masked it,' which is the actual business question.
suggested_addition: Make the decision rule explicit and domain-meaningful: state up front that if the best rung fails AC1 AND the train/eval gap is large (undertraining signature), the canonical next step is to re-run the SAME structured encoder with weight-carryover before concluding locality doesn't help — so the negative result triggers the right follow-up rather than abandoning the encoder hypothesis.

the recorded v2
diagnosis (memory + RESULTS.md) attributes the Medium regression to *undertraining* under that regime, warning
that *adding capacity could make Medium worse*

the structured encoder BEATS the v3 rich-pool on
**Medium** at **p<0.05**

title: owner_slot 255→41 reindex must preserve 'self vs civ vs none' semantics consistently
description: The brief reindexes owner_slot 255→41 across spatial ch7/ch10 and unit/city owner_slot, sharing a slot table of size 42 (0 none, 1..40 civ, 41 self). But the domain meaning of slot 0 differs by channel: spatial ch7 owner=0 means 'no owner / unowned tile', while unit owner_slot is always a real civ (units always belong to someone). Collapsing these into one shared embedding table risks the model conflating 'unowned tile' with 'unknown/missing unit owner'.
rationale: Ownership is a core domain concept with distinct meanings (unowned territory vs. an enemy unit's owner); a shared table that overloads index 0 across semantically different fields can blur a distinction the policy needs to reason about.
suggested_addition: Document the slot-table semantics per field: confirm whether index 0 means the same thing for tile-owner (unowned) and unit-owner (sentinel/absent), and either justify the shared table or note that the embedding learns the field-context from the surrounding token. Add an assertion that unit owner_slot is never 0 for a present unit.
source_excerpts:
owner_slot & unit_owner_slot
share a **slot table size 42** (`maxCivTokens 40 +2`; reindex 255→41)
7 owner_slot (0 none, 1..N civ, 255 self)
